design_philosophy.core_loop: Input personal data (name, birth date, gender) → pose a question → receive tarot reading via Celtic cross layout → receive lottery numbers based on selected US state
design_philosophy.primary_tension: Supernatural prediction vs. empirical skepticism; marketed mystique vs. disclaimer of entertainment-only purpose
design_philosophy.player_fantasy: Genuine divination and fate-telling; experiencing authentic tarot reading mechanics with personal stakes (lottery predictions)
design_philosophy.what_makes_it_opinionated: Rare's deliberate choice to create a non-game within gaming platform constraints. Refuses traditional gameplay loops entirely—no win conditions, no skill-based challenge, no failure states. The game asserts that divination simulation and spectacle constitute valid interactive entertainment. Includes nudity and religious imagery explicitly prohibited under NOA guidelines, suggesting intentional boundary-pushing. The famous 'oracle warning' unused graphic ('DO NOT TEST THE ORACLE') indicates original vision leaned into supernatural authenticity fiction before revision.

difficulty.player_agency_in_difficulty: No difficulty mechanics exist. Player agency limited to input questions and selecting lottery state. Outcome generation is deterministic algorithm, not challenge-based.

replayability_hooks: Unlimited tarot readings with different questions
Different lottery number predictions per US state
Repeated use as social party game activity
Variable card outcomes (normal/reversed combinations from 78-card deck)

world_building_techniques: Instruction booklet provides historical origins of tarot word and concept
Booklet catalogs all Major and Minor Arcana with symbolic meanings
Celtic cross layout positions carry thematic significance for fortune interpretation
Framing device: personal data input (birth date, gender) creates sense of individualized prophecy

faction_indicators: Skeptics dismissing game as entertainment algorithm
danger_signals: Urban legend: game allegedly predicted deaths of young players accurately, creating superstitious fear
Age-gated content (14+) with religious imagery and nudity violated NOA norms
Dual warnings (age restriction + entertainment-only disclaimer) signaled taboo subject matter to 1989 audience
Title positioning as 'oracle' rather than 'game' implied supernatural authority
environmental_storytelling: Title screen revisions show publisher concern with legitimacy (trademark/copyright symbol placement)
Unused blood droplet graphic and 'BEWARE: DO NOT TEST THE ORACLE' warning indicate darker original vision
NES platform itself subverted as divination tool rather than entertainment device

sources_used: tcrf (The Cutting Room Floor) - Taboo: The Sixth Sense article
wikipedia - Taboo: The Sixth Sense article
